name: scientific-visualization
description: Meta-skill for publication-ready figures. Use when creating journal submission figures requiring multi-panel layouts, significance annotations, error bars, colorblind-safe palettes, and specific journal formatting (Nature, Science, Cell). Orchestrates matplotlib/seaborn/plotly with publication styles. For quick exploration use seaborn or plotly directly.
capabilities: Generate publication-ready figures with proper DPI and vector formats
Apply journal-specific styling for Nature, Science, Cell, PLOS, and IEEE
Create multi-panel figures with consistent styling
Add statistical annotations and error bars correctly
Export figures in PDF, EPS, SVG, TIFF, and PNG formats
author: K-Dense-AI
securitySummary: All 309 static findings are false positives. The scanner misidentifies hex color codes (#E69F00, etc.) as cryptographic hashes, markdown code blocks as shell execution, and configuration variables as certificate files. This is a legitimate scientific visualization library with matplotlib styling, color palettes, and figure export utilities. No actual security risks exist - the skill only manipulates local figure files and contains no network calls, external commands, or credential handling.
valueStatement: Scientific visualization transforms data into clear, accurate figures for publication. This skill ensures your figures meet journal standards while being accessible to all readers, including those with color blindness.
limitations: Requires matplotlib, seaborn, and plotly installations
Focused on 2D static figures for print publication
Journal specifications should be verified before submission

useCases.title: Create Journal-Quality Figures
useCases.description: Generate publication-ready figures that meet specific journal requirements for manuscripts and grant applications

useCases.title: Learn Scientific Visualization
useCases.description: Master best practices for creating clear, accessible figures for theses and presentations

useCases.title: Standardize Figure Production
useCases.description: Automate figure generation with consistent styling across multiple projects and teams

promptTemplates.title: Basic Publication Plot
promptTemplates.scenario: Create a simple line plot for a paper
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a publication-quality line plot of my data with error bars, proper labels with units, and save as PDF. Use the Okabe-Ito color palette for accessibility.

promptTemplates.title: Multi-Panel Figure
promptTemplates.scenario: Combine multiple plots into one figure
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a 4-panel figure (2x2 layout) comparing different experimental conditions. Label panels A-D, use consistent styling, and ensure the figure fits Nature's single column width.

promptTemplates.title: Statistical Comparison
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a box plot comparing three treatment groups with individual data points overlaid. Add significance markers and use colorblind-safe colors. Include sample sizes.

promptTemplates.title: Journal-Specific Formatting
promptTemplates.prompt: Configure matplotlib for Cell journal requirements and create a heatmap with proper colormap. Ensure the figure meets their dimension and font requirements.

bestPractices: Always test figures in grayscale to ensure accessibility without color
Include sample sizes (n) and error bar definitions in figure captions
Use vector formats (PDF, EPS) for plots; use TIFF only for images
antiPatterns: Never use JPEG format for scientific figures - it creates compression artifacts
Avoid red-green color combinations that are invisible to 8% of men with color blindness
Do not use 3D effects or unnecessary decorations that distort data perception

faq.question: What file format should I use for my figures?
faq.answer: Use vector formats (PDF or EPS) for plots and graphs. Use TIFF or PNG for microscopy images. Never use JPEG for scientific figures.

faq.question: How do I ensure my figures are colorblind-friendly?
faq.answer: Use the built-in Okabe-Ito palette, test figures in grayscale, and add redundant encoding like line styles or markers.

faq.question: What is the minimum font size for publication figures?
faq.answer: At final print size, use minimum 7-9 pt for axis labels and 6-8 pt for tick labels. Arial or Helvetica work best.

faq.question: How do I create multi-panel figures?
faq.answer: Use matplotlib GridSpec for flexible layouts, label panels with bold letters, and ensure consistent styling across panels.

faq.question: Can I use this for any journal?
faq.answer: Yes, includes configurations for Nature, Science, Cell, PLOS, ACS, and IEEE. Custom dimensions can be set for other journals.

faq.question: What statistical information should I include?
faq.answer: Always show error bars (specify SD, SEM, or CI), include sample sizes (n), and mark statistical significance clearly.
